subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Olbers' Paradox: Why Is the Sky Dark?
description: A short lesson to help you apply Olbers' Paradox: Why Is the Sky Dark?.

hook.text: If the universe is infinite and full of stars, the sky should be bright everywhere. So why is it dark at night?

buildup.text: Heinrich Olbers posed this in 1823. In an infinite, eternal universe, every sightline should end at a star.

discovery.text: The answer is that the universe has a finite age. Light from the most distant stars hasn't reached us yet.

twist.text: Also, the expansion of space stretches distant starlight into invisible infrared and microwave wavelengths.

climax.text: The dark night sky is itself evidence that the universe had a beginning and is expanding. Darkness tells a story.

punchline.text: A dark sky proves the universe isn't infinite or eternal. Darkness speaks.

quiz.question: Why is the night sky dark if there are countless stars?
options: The universe has a finite age, so light from distant stars hasn't reached us yet
Most stars are too dim to be seen from Earth
Interstellar dust blocks nearly all starlight
